planting.note: Start transplants indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost or divide existing plants in early spring; plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — blue wood aster is the most shade-tolerant of the native asters and grows naturally in the dim understory of mature deciduous forests.

directions: Start transplants indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost or divide existing plants in early spring; plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — blue wood aster is the most shade-tolerant of the native asters and grows naturally in the dim understory of mature deciduous forests. Symphyotrichum cordifolium is invaluable as one of very few native plants that blooms with full vigor in deep shade from September through October, producing 1/2-inch, star-shaped, lavender-blue to violet-blue flowers by the thousands on freely branching stems when shade-garden foliage plants have lost their interest. In autumn it creates a breathtaking display of blue beneath the yellow fall leaves of maples and beeches; it is a critical nectar source for late-season migrating monarchs and native bees in woodland settings where other asters cannot grow. The plant self-seeds and gradually naturalizes into stunning woodland colonies.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun; calico aster self-seeds readily in suitable conditions and gradually naturalizes into graceful colonies at woodland margins.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun; calico aster self-seeds readily in suitable conditions and gradually naturalizes into graceful colonies at woodland margins. Symphyotrichum lateriflorum is a graceful, fine-textured native aster that produces arching stems bearing tiny (3/8-inch), white to pale-lavender flowers with yellow centers that quickly turn red-purple, creating a calico-like pattern of white and reddish-purple flowers on the same stem from late August through October; the foliage often turns bronzy-purple in fall, and the plant is outstanding in the winter garden when the dried stems and reddened berries provide fine texture and food for birds. Extremely adaptable to partial shade, dry conditions, and poor soils where other asters fail; often listed as the best aster for difficult, dry-shade sites.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp aster is native to wet meadows, streambanks, and open swampy areas and requires consistently moist to wet soil — excellent in rain gardens and bog borders.

directions: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp aster is native to wet meadows, streambanks, and open swampy areas and requires consistently moist to wet soil — excellent in rain gardens and bog borders. Symphyotrichum puniceum is one of the tallest and showiest native fall asters, producing large (1–1.5 inch), vivid violet to purple ray flowers with orange-yellow centers on reddish-purple, somewhat hairy stems in September and October; the stems and leaves have a distinctive reddish to wine-purple color that extends the ornamental season beyond bloom. It is native to wet, open habitats from Newfoundland to Georgia and is one of the most vigorous and cold-hardy fall asters for moist conditions, supporting monarch butterflies, bumblebees, and specialist aster bees during the critical fall migration and foraging period.

planting.note: Start transplants indoors or divide existing plants in spring; plant in very well-drained, dry, sandy to rocky, slightly acidic to neutral, poor to average soil in full sun; stiff-leaf aster is adapted to dry, infertile conditions and will become weedy and floppy in rich, moist soil.

directions: Start transplants indoors or divide existing plants in spring; plant in very well-drained, dry, sandy to rocky, slightly acidic to neutral, poor to average soil in full sun; stiff-leaf aster is adapted to dry, infertile conditions and will become weedy and floppy in rich, moist soil. Ionactis linariifolius is a compact, fine-textured native aster of rocky outcrops, sandy barrens, and dry acidic woodlands, with stiff, needle-like, linear leaves (hence linariifolius) and a profusion of small, 3/4-inch, bright violet-blue to lavender flowers with yellow centers in September and October; it is one of the most drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant of the native asters, thriving in lean sandy soils and rocky barrens where most asters cannot establish. An excellent choice for dry, naturalistic rock gardens and sandy pine barrens-style gardens; it is a specialist larval host for the scalloped sallow moth and provides nectar for specialist aster bees.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or divisions in spring in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; flat-topped aster spreads by rhizomes to form large colonies in suitable moist conditions and can be aggressive in small gardens — use it in large native plantings and rain gardens where spread is welcome.

directions: Plant container-grown transplants or divisions in spring in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; flat-topped aster spreads by rhizomes to form large colonies in suitable moist conditions and can be aggressive in small gardens — use it in large native plantings and rain gardens where spread is welcome. Doellingeria umbellata is a robust, tall native perennial of moist meadows, wet prairies, streambanks, and open shrubby swamps throughout eastern North America, producing many-branched stems with large, flat-topped to umbellate clusters of numerous small, white to pale lavender-white flowers with yellow centers from August through October. The flat-topped flower clusters are exceptional landing pads for pollinating insects — the flowers attract an extraordinary diversity of native bees, wasps, flies, and butterflies, providing an important late-season nectar resource as most other wildflowers decline. It spreads vigorously by rhizomes and is best used in large-scale naturalistic plantings rather than formal borders.
